---
title: "Writing a 62,500-Word Book with Agent Fleets"
number-sections: false
---

**How Copilot CLI orchestrated 11 agent personas across 50+ dispatches to produce *The Agentic SDLC Handbook* — using the methodology the book itself teaches.**

::: {.callout-tip title="Key Takeaways — The TL;DR"}
- The same primitives that orchestrate code changes — personas, skills, file-scoped rules — orchestrate prose at book scale.
- Wave ordering, checkpoint discipline, and context budgeting matter more than model capability.
- Dynamic persona creation mid-project beats freezing the team at inception — three of eleven personas were created in response to gaps the process surfaced.
- When agents unanimously agree, a single human override can still be the correct call (the `apm compile` veto).
- Composition is load-bearing: 15 chapters required 50+ dispatches, 4 waves, and 2 integration passes. No single agent could hold the full manuscript.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
This session used GitHub Copilot CLI as the orchestrator, but the patterns apply to any agent-capable environment.
:::

---

## The Meta-Narrative

This is a case study about a book that was written using the process it describes. *The Agentic SDLC Handbook* is a 15-chapter, 62,500-word technical book on AI-native software development. It was authored entirely through agentic orchestration in a single Copilot CLI session. The human author — Daniel Meppiel, Global Black Belt at Microsoft and creator of APM — provided domain expertise, intellectual property context, and editorial direction. Copilot CLI managed the agent team.

The agent team was packaged and distributed using APM itself: 8 persona definitions as `.agent.md` files, an orchestration workflow as `SKILL.md`, declared as a dependency in `apm.yml`, and deployed via `apm install`. APM was used to build the book that teaches about APM.

This case study maps the execution to the handbook's own vocabulary (Chapter 14) and tests its structural properties (Chapter 15) under real conditions.

---

## Team Topology: 11 Personas, Four Pods

The orchestrator designed an 8-persona expert panel at project inception, then dynamically created 3 more as needs emerged during execution. The personas organized into four functional pods:

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5.5
graph TB
    subgraph Editorial["Editorial Pod"]
        CE[Chief Editor<br/>Cross-chapter coherence]
        TLA[Thought Leadership Advisor<br/>Positioning & differentiation]
    end

    subgraph Domain["Domain Expert Pod"]
        CS[C-Suite Strategist<br/>Executive lens, ROI framing]
        PA[Practitioner Authority<br/>Engineering credibility]
        MA[Market Analyst<br/>Competitive landscape]
        PS[Platform Strategist<br/>Architecture patterns]
    end

    subgraph Review["Review Pod"]
        CTO[CTO Proxy<br/>"Would I buy this for my team?"]
        DL[Dev Lead Proxy<br/>"Would my engineers use this?"]
    end

    subgraph Audit["Audit Pod — Dynamically Created"]
        IL[Illustrator &#35;9<br/>Visual strategist]
        FC[Fact-Ref-Checker &#35;10<br/>Claims auditor]
        PB[Publishing Advisor &#35;11<br/>Distribution strategy]
    end

    CE --> CTO
    CE --> DL
    CE --> IL
    CE --> FC

    style Editorial fill:#2d4a7a,color:#fff
    style Domain fill:#4a6741,color:#fff
    style Review fill:#7a5c2d,color:#fff
    style Audit fill:#6b3a5c,color:#fff
```

Each persona was a *primitive* — a composable instruction unit defined in a single `.agent.md` file. The orchestrator never prompted agents as generic LLMs; every dispatch carried persona context, scope boundaries, and output format requirements.

---

## Execution Timeline: Four Waves Plus Integration

The manuscript was produced in a phased pipeline: corpus audit → architecture → four writing waves → integration review → polish. Each wave followed checkpoint discipline: draft, review, revise, commit.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5.5
gantt
    title Manuscript Production Pipeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s

    section Phase 0
    Corpus Audit (4 parallel specialists)    :done, p0, 0, 1
    Chief Editor Synthesis                    :done, p0s, 1, 2

    section Phase 1
    Architecture Attempt 1 (FAILED)          :crit, p1f, 2, 3
    Architecture Split (2 parallel)          :done, p1, 3, 4

    section Wave 0
    Ch1 Draft                                :done, w0d, 4, 5
    3 Parallel Reviews                       :done, w0r, 5, 6
    Revision (7 fixes)                       :done, w0v, 6, 7

    section Wave 1
    4 Parallel Drafts (Ch2,10,11,14)         :done, w1d, 7, 8
    4 Parallel Reviews                       :done, w1r, 8, 9
    4 Parallel Revisions                     :done, w1v, 9, 10

    section Wave 2
    5 Parallel Drafts (Ch5,7,8,9,13)         :done, w2d, 10, 11
    2 Combined Reviews                       :done, w2r, 11, 12
    5 Parallel Revisions                     :done, w2v, 12, 13

    section Wave 3
    5 Parallel Drafts (Ch3,4,6,12,15)        :done, w3d, 13, 14
    2 Combined Reviews                       :done, w3r, 14, 15
    2 Parallel Revisions                     :done, w3v, 15, 16

    section Integration
    Block 1+2 Reviews (parallel)             :done, ir, 16, 17
    2 Parallel Fix Agents                    :done, fix, 17, 18
    Polish + README                          :done, pol, 18, 19
```

**Wave ordering was deliberate.** Wave 0 tested the pipeline with a single chapter. Wave 1 targeted chapters with the most source material (lowest risk). Wave 2 took on the hardest chapters requiring fresh writing. Wave 3 handled integration chapters that needed cross-references to earlier work. This is *context budgeting* — right-sizing each wave's scope to what the agents could handle with available context.

---

## The Draft→Review→Revise Cycle

Every chapter passed through an identical three-stage pipeline. This is the core quality loop the book describes in Chapter 13.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5.5
sequenceDiagram
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant D as Draft Agent<br/>(Domain Specialist)
    participant R1 as CTO Proxy
    participant R2 as Dev Lead Proxy
    participant R3 as Chief Editor
    participant V as Revision Agent

    O->>D: Dispatch: Write chapter N<br/>(architecture spec + source material)
    D-->>O: Chapter draft (3,000-5,000 words)

    par Parallel Review
        O->>R1: Review for executive audience
        O->>R2: Review for practitioner audience
        O->>R3: Review for coherence + voice
    end

    R1-->>O: REVISE verdict + fixes
    R2-->>O: REVISE verdict + fixes
    R3-->>O: REVISE verdict + fixes

    O->>O: Synthesize consensus fixes
    O->>V: Dispatch: Apply N fixes to chapter
    V-->>O: Revised chapter
    O->>O: Checkpoint: commit draft + revision
```

In later waves, the orchestrator batched reviews by persona rather than by chapter — sending one reviewer all 5 chapters at once rather than dispatching 15 separate reviews. This reduced dispatch overhead without sacrificing coverage, a practical application of *reduced scope* at the orchestration level.

---

## Dynamic Persona Creation

Three of the eleven personas did not exist at project inception. They were created in response to gaps the process itself surfaced.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 5.5
flowchart TD
    A[Integration Review<br/>Chief Editor reads all 15 chapters] --> B{Visual anchors<br/>missing?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Create Illustrator Agent &#35;9<br/>Visual Strategist persona]
    C --> D[Fleet of 2: Block 1 + Block 2<br/>40 visual opportunities found<br/>15 Mermaid diagrams embedded]

    A --> E{Inconsistent statistics<br/>across chapters?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Create Fact-Ref-Checker &#35;10<br/>Claims Auditor persona]
    F --> G[Fleet of 2: 75 flags found<br/>5 critical — including PR &#35;394<br/>statistic contradiction]

    H[Question: How do we publish?] --> I{Publishing expertise<br/>needed?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Create Publishing Advisor &#35;11<br/>5 publishing paths evaluated]
    J --> K[Recommended Open Core +<br/>Premium model]

    style C fill:#6b3a5c,color:#fff
    style F fill:#6b3a5c,color:#fff
    style J fill:#6b3a5c,color:#fff
```

This validates the handbook's claim that primitives should be created and iterated throughout a project, not frozen at inception. Anti-pattern #10 (*Not Fixing the Primitives*) warns against correcting symptoms manually instead of updating the instruction set. Each dynamic persona was the fix — a new primitive that addressed a structural gap rather than a one-off patch.

---

## Escalation Events and Anti-Pattern Mapping

Four escalation events interrupted normal flow. Each maps to a specific anti-pattern from Chapter 14.

### 1. Architecture Agent Timeout

**What happened:** A single agent was dispatched to produce the full 15-chapter architecture. It failed after 26 minutes — a connection error caused by context window exhaustion.

**Anti-pattern:** #11 (*Context Window Exhaustion*) compounded by #6 (*The Solo Hero*). The orchestrator assigned monolithic scope to one agent, violating the *one file, one agent per wave* isolation principle.

**Resolution:** Split into two parallel agents — Part 1 (Chapters 1–8), Part 2 (Chapters 9–15 plus cross-cutting concerns). Both completed successfully, producing 1,020 lines of chapter specifications.

**What held true:** *Context will remain finite and fragile.* Regardless of model capability, there is always a limit to how much an agent can consider effectively.

### 2. Chief Editor Synthesis Scope

**What happened:** After four corpus audit specialists completed their parallel scans, their outputs needed cross-cutting synthesis that no individual specialist could produce — each had domain-specific findings but none had the full picture.

**Anti-pattern:** #1 (*Monolithic Prompt*) — the temptation was to dump all four outputs into one specialist agent. Instead, the orchestrator escalated to the Chief Editor persona, whose explicit role was cross-chapter coherence.

**Resolution:** Chief Editor ran as synthesizer, producing 7 consensus themes, 6 resolved tensions, 10 cuts, and 9 identified gaps. The persona's scope matched the task.

**What held true:** *Composition will remain necessary.* Complex analysis required coordination across specialists and structured integration of results.

### 3. User Vetoed `apm compile`

**What happened:** During the APM strategic insertion phase, the orchestrator prepared six surgical insertions. The human author intervened: "Do not mention `apm compile` — niche feature." All six insertions were adapted to respect the constraint.

**Anti-pattern:** #17 (*Persona Drift*) — without the human override, the agents would have promoted a technical feature that violated the book's editorial stance. The author's judgment caught what no reviewer persona was calibrated to flag.

**What held true:** *Human judgment remains the bottleneck and the differentiator.* The scarce resource was not token generation but the ability to decide what the book should and should not say.

### 4. Panel Disagreement on APM Prominence

**What happened:** After the "zero APM mentions in 62,500 words" discovery, the Market Analyst wanted more visibility; the Thought Leadership Advisor wanted less. The panel genuinely disagreed.

**Anti-pattern:** #15 (*The "Almost Done" Trap*) — the temptation was to skip the synthesis step and let one voice dominate. Instead, the Chief Editor resolved with a governing principle: *"The book is 100% useful without APM. APM appears as proof, not prerequisite."*

**Resolution:** 6 surgical insertions, 5 name-mentions, approximately 475 words across 5 chapters. The lightest possible touch.

**What held true:** *Explicit knowledge is more valuable than implicit knowledge.* The governing principle, once articulated, made every subsequent decision mechanical.

---

## The APM Zero-Mentions Embarrassment

A book about AI-native software development, written by APM's creator, using APM's orchestration infrastructure, contained zero mentions of APM across 62,500 words.

This was not a bug. It was the methodology working correctly. Each agent was dispatched with chapter-specific source material and audience-appropriate scope. No agent had "promote the author's project" in its persona. The absence proved the review process was honest — and prompted a four-wave strategic assessment:

1. **Wave 1:** 4 specialist audits (Market Analyst, Platform Strategist, Practitioner Authority, Publishing Advisor)
2. **Wave 2:** Chief Editor + Thought Leadership Advisor synthesis
3. **Wave 3:** CTO Proxy + Dev Lead Proxy validation
4. **Wave 4:** Final consolidated report

The result was surgical rather than promotional. Credibility comes from restraint.

---

## What Held True Regardless of the Model

Chapter 15 identifies five properties that hold regardless of model improvement. This project tested all five under real conditions:

| Property | How This Project Tested It |
|---|---|
| **Context will remain finite and fragile** | Architecture agent failed at monolithic scope. Every wave was sized to context budget. |
| **Output will remain probabilistic** | Three-reviewer pipeline caught inconsistencies no single review pass would find. Fact-checker found 75 flags. |
| **Explicit knowledge > implicit knowledge** | "Proof, not prerequisite" principle — once articulated — resolved all APM insertion decisions mechanically. |
| **Human judgment remains bottleneck and differentiator** | User vetoed `apm compile`, overriding all 11 agent personas. Correct call that no agent was positioned to make. |
| **Composition will remain necessary** | 15 chapters required 50+ dispatches, 4 waves, 3 dynamically created personas, and 2 integration review passes. No single agent could hold the full manuscript in focus. |

The constraints are not training wheels. They are load-bearing architecture.

---

::: {.callout-note title="Metrics"}
62,510 words · 15 chapters · 11 personas (8 + 3 dynamic) · ~50+ dispatches · 4 writing waves + integration + polish · 15 Mermaid diagrams · 75 fact-check flags (5 critical) · 40 visual opportunities · 5 APM mentions (~475 words) · 4 anti-patterns encountered · 5/5 structural properties tested
:::

---

## What This Proves

The Agentic SDLC methodology generalizes beyond code — the same primitives, discipline, and composition patterns that manage multi-agent code changes managed a multi-chapter editorial workflow. The methodology survived contact with its own subject matter. See the TL;DR at the top for the distilled lessons.
